topic: Permissible Acts, Breaking, and Abandoning the Prayer
title: Ruling on Abandoning the Prayer out of Laziness
short_rule: Hanbalis, Malikis, and Shafi'is rule that one who abandons prayer out of laziness is executed, whereas Hanafis rule he is imprisoned and beaten.
details: There is a severe penalty for intentionally abandoning the obligatory prayer out of laziness (without denying its obligation). The Hanbali, Maliki, and Shafi'i schools maintain that the person is asked to repent for three days; if they refuse, they are subject to capital punishment. In contrast, the Hanafi school rules that such a person is not executed but is instead beaten until they bleed and imprisoned until they resume praying.
actionable_steps: Recognize the severe spiritual and legal consequences of neglecting the prayer.
quran_hadith_evidence: Derived from Prophetic warnings that the covenant distinguishing a believer from disbelief is the establishment of the ritual prayer.
spiritual_wisdom: The prayer is the foundational pillar of Islam and the primary link between the servant and the Creator; abandoning it severs this tie and threatens the integrity of one's entire faith.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn Qudamah

topic: Permissible Acts, Breaking, and Abandoning the Prayer
title: Mandatory and Permissible Breaking of the Prayer
short_rule: It is mandatory to break the prayer for emergencies, and permissible to break it to protect significant property.
details: The Hanafi school explicitly outlines situations where breaking an obligatory prayer is permitted or required. It is mandatory (Wajib) to break the prayer if one hears a call for help in an emergency, such as someone in distress or a blind person about to fall into a well. It is permissible to break the prayer to prevent the theft of property worth at least one dirham, or for a shepherd fearing a wolf attack on his flock. Midwives may also delay or interrupt their prayer if they fear for the life of the mother or child during delivery.
actionable_steps: Break your prayer immediately if someone's life or safety is in immediate danger.
You may legally break your prayer if you are being robbed of something valuable.
A midwife should prioritize a delivery over praying on time if there is fear of harm.
quran_hadith_evidence: Preserving human life is a primary objective (maqsad) of the Sacred Law, superseding the immediate completion of a ritual act.
spiritual_wisdom: Islam balances the utmost reverence for divine worship with the sanctity of human life and the preservation of property, reflecting God's mercy and practicality.
author: Al-Shurunbulali

topic: Permissible Acts, Breaking, and Abandoning the Prayer
title: Killing a Snake or Scorpion During Prayer
short_rule: It is permissible to kill a snake or scorpion during the prayer to prevent harm.
details: The Hanafi school categorizes killing a dangerous pest, such as a snake or a scorpion, as permissible during the prayer, provided one does so with no more than two strikes, even if it requires turning away from the Qibla. Doing so does not invalidate the prayer.
actionable_steps: If approached by a dangerous creature like a scorpion while praying, you may kill it.
Ensure the action is swift and minimal (no more than two strikes) to avoid excessive movement.
quran_hadith_evidence: Derived from the Prophet's command allowing the killing of the 'two black ones' (the snake and the scorpion) even during the ritual prayer.
spiritual_wisdom: The Sacred Law does not expect the worshipper to endure physical harm or paralyzing distress while praying; it provides practical concessions to ensure physical safety.
author: Al-Shurunbulali

topic: Permissible Acts, Breaking, and Abandoning the Prayer
title: Minor Extraneous Movements
short_rule: Minor movements for a valid need are permissible and do not invalidate the prayer.
details: While excessive movements invalidate the prayer, minor movements are permissible (Mubah) and excused. The Hanbali text notes that the Prophet carried his granddaughter Umamah during prayer and opened the door for Aisha, indicating that small, necessary movements do not harm the prayer. The Hanafi school similarly permits strapping a sword, fastening a belt, or praying facing someone's back (even if they are quietly speaking), provided the movement is not distracting or excessive.
actionable_steps: You may perform a very minor movement if a sudden need arises, such as taking a few steps to open a nearby door or holding a child.
Keep all extraneous movements to an absolute minimum to preserve Khushu' (reverence).
quran_hadith_evidence: The Prophet carried Umamah bint Zainab during the prayer, putting her down when he prostrated and picking her up when he stood. (Agreed Upon)
spiritual_wisdom: Demonstrates the profound integration of daily human life, empathy, and family responsibilities within the framework of divine worship.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn Qudamah
